candidates.stmt: I was elected to Mole Valley District Council two years ago and have been an active councillor, championing Ashtead’s interests and working hard for constituents and community groups. I am now seeking election to the new East Surrey Unitary Council. to continue this work in the reorganised local government system in Surrey. I am passionate about protecting our local countryside and Green Belt, and preserving the rural character of our village and surrounding areas. I have been an Ashtead resident for more than 30 years, but originally from neighbouring Leatherhead and so I have known this area well all my life. Most weekends I can be found walking in the local countryside with my dog and grandchildren! I am a journalist by profession, currently self-employed as a writer and editor, and am active in a number of local and national charities in the following areas: countryside protection, heritage, wildlife & animal welfare, the journalistic profession, and armed forces veterans, serving as a charity trustee and volunteer.

Alison Kelly has lived in Epsom since 1986 and her daughter attended Rosebery. Starting work teaching maths, she moved to computing and is now an IT Manager.

Alison is well known in Town ward, which she represented on the council from 2007 to 2015, when she was the opposition spokesperson on budgets and finance.

She became involved in local politics due to her dismay at the number of planning decisions that had depleted open areas and back garden spaces. So she welcomed the opportunity to be directly involved in deciding local planning issues.

Alison's particular interests are in the areas of energy efficiency and recycling. In her spare time she enjoys going to the gym, cycling, gardening and baking cakes.

I grew up on Epsom Downs, attended school & sixth form at Therfield school and now live in Leatherhead with my fiancée.

Ashtead has always been close to my heart with many friends and memories residing there. I have been involved with various sporting clubs and activities, especially swimming and judo.

For work, I have been employed across Mole Valley in various management roles in the leisure industry. More recently I worked as a store manager & operationally across leisure and property sectors.

I am standing for Reform UK because I have seen our country and communities degenerate due to poor management and leadership. It does not feel like the fantastic society of even a few years ago. I want to protect our green spaces and foster that community feeling through our culture.

I have been a resident of Ashtead for 26 years and a Green Party member for 5 years. This is a beautiful area to live in and I am passionate about protecting nature and our green spaces. I have been involved with a few local charities supporting the most vulnerable people. As your councillor, I will fight for better community cohesion and greater compassion and understanding of those in need. I am married to a womble and so you may see me litter picking around the area!

I have lived in Ashtead all my life, and been a member of the Green Party for 4 years. I've always loved Ashtead, from its town to its community and greenery. I want to do what I can to keep Ashtead thriving, from supporting local shops and businesses to seeing the Common flourish. I work as an artist, teaching children art lessons as well as producing comics. Working with young people and encouraging their creativity makes me want to do what I can to see these children have an amazing future. I'll fight for making everyone's future as bright and green as possible.

My family has lived in Ashtead for over a hundred years. I am a retired musician who has lived in Ashtead for most of my life. I am married with two adult children who attended local state schools. I am a member of Leatherhead & District Local History Society, the Surrey Philharmonic Orchestra, Chairman of Dorking Concert goers and a volunteer driver for Ashtead Good Neighbours. If elected my priorities will be to improve social care, the condition of roads and pavements, to protect the Green Belt and to reduce fly tipping by reducing fees charged to businesses.

I have lived and worked in this area my entire life. From my time as a Scout Leader to serving as a local school governor, I am deeply rooted in our community. I became a Councillor to give back to the place I call home, and I am now seeking to bring my 10 years of experience to represent Banstead, Woodmansterne and Chipstead on the new East Surrey Council.

**A Track Record of Delivery**
While serving on Reigate & Banstead Borough Council, I have focused on tangible improvements for residents. I am proud to have delivered:

- **Recreational Facilities**: Secured funding for a new outdoor gym, a community boules pitch, and a new skate park.
- **Infrastructure Improvements**: Helped secure funding for a 5G football pitch and a new car park for a local sports club, alongside supporting various small-scale community projects.
- **Modernising Services**: As a member of the Executive, I oversaw a vital update of the Council’s IT equipment to ensure more efficient and reliable services for residents.
- **Environmental Protection**: Led the successful planning refusal of a proposed crematorium in Reigate, preventing the loss of valuable community open space.
- **Supporting our Armed Forces**: Successfully pushed the Council to achieve the **Defence Employer Recognition Scheme Silver Accreditation**, ensuring we properly support those who serve.

Professional Expertise for our New Council
As an Actuary who has worked at Just in Reigate and Legal & General in Kingswood, I bring a high level of financial scrutiny to local government. Having served on the Executive with responsibility for Policy and Performance, I have ensured the Council remains well-run and accountable. I want to ensure the new East Surrey Council starts with a foundation of fiscal discipline by:

- **Balancing the Budget**: Replicating a strong record of financial responsibility.
- **Protecting Services**: Ensuring above and beyond services remain a priority.
- **Lowering Tax Pressure**: Keeping council tax

Born in Leeds in 1966 Anthony has worked for 35 years in the record industry. He has lived and worked in Redhill since 1999 and his hobbies include golf, theatre and literature. His chief political priorities are environmental sustainability and education. He believes the 2025 county council elections give Surrey residents the chance to  reshape the services afforded to businesses and residents within Surrey, enhancing  local government support to meet their changing needs at a time of financial uncertainty. With the 2023 net zero target firmly in mind, the Council needs to  rise to  the imminent environmental challenges  with a focus on green energy and improving air quality.

Having joined the Lib Dems whilst studying politics at Reigate College, Trixie is keen to get further involved in local politics, helping the people of Banstead, Woodmansterne and Chipstead. Being a Young Liberal candidate, Trixie is perfectly placed to work with all generations to improve East Surrey. As well as being an active campaigner, Trixie is working towards a BSc in Natural Sciences.

Caroline is a local mother and small business owner, who is committed to making a positive change in her community. With her experience and dedication to helping her own children with SEN, she understands the importance of supporting families and creating opportunities for all. As a member of the Liberal Democrats, Caroline believes in the power of collaboration and finding common ground to tackle the pressing issues facing our society. With Caroline's passion for progress and dedication to inclusive policies, she is the ideal candidate to represent the interests of the people of Fetcham.

I am a comedy writer, performer and producer for television and radio. I have written for Have I Got News For You, The News Quiz, Gigglebiz, have been on two BAFTA-winning writing teams and I was Head Writer on Bad Dinosaurs for Netflix. I have lived in Dorking and East Surrey for twenty years and have brought my children up in the local schools (North Downs Primary and The Ashcombe). Both my father and grandfather were ministers at Leatherhead United Reformed Church. I am a company founder, and an Associate Fellow of the Royal Historical Society. I regularly perform at Comedy At The Cottage in Redhill, and have spent a lot of time playing fake politicians at fundraisers and on Channel 4’s Tonightly. I currently run an animation production company, and live with his wife, children, dog, and cat.

Ben has lived in Caterham for most of his life, apart from a brief stint at the University of Edinburgh. Going to school on the Hill and in the Valley, Ben has grown up with Caterham as it has changed, sometimes slowly and sometimes rapidly over the last 30 years. Elected for the first time in 2024, Ben serves as a Parish and District Councillor for Caterham Hill, and now leads his group on the District Council. He is standing as a candidate for the East Surrey Unitary, Caterham Hill Division.

The changes coming to Surrey and to Caterham & Chaldon through Local Government Reorganisation will be big, and Ben is keenly aware of the effect this will have on residents and services. The new Unitary Council will have on day one big challenges, and Ben is determined to make sure that Caterham & Chaldon residents don’t pay for other councils past mistakes. While the 2 parishes might be on the edge of East Surrey, Ben will make sure their voice is heard when it matters.

As a councillor, Ben has worked hard at both parish and district councils to bring local assets, such as the Westway Common and Westway & Heath Road allotments back under parish control with greater resident access and oversight. When it comes to amenities in our community, Ben firmly believes that Caterham & Chaldon residents know best what to do and how to run our own assets which we all rely on.

Caterham Hill & Chaldon have many areas of local unspoilt natural beauty, and Ben will be a fierce champion in preserving the Green Belt in our communities. Housing development targets from Westminster and speculative property developers cannot come at the expense of the parts of countryside that make living and raising a family here so wholesome, and Ben will fight to make sure that does not change.

Martin is Caterham through and through. Growing up in Caterham, attending schools in Caterham, running businesses in Caterham all his life, Martin is well known in the business and local community of Caterham and serves as a District Councillor for Westway, Caterham-on-the-Hill. He is now standing as a candidate for the new East Surrey Council, Caterham-on-the-Hill Division.

Listening to residents’ concerns, Martin has worked to improve and implement better planning controls to help protect residential neighbourhoods for local residents. His depth of local knowledge is combined with a deep understanding of community values and how important it is to protect our area and our facilities. Martin recognises that when changes are made they must benefit the community and provide better outcomes for residents.

From his knowledge of planning and design, he understands why existing roads and neighbourhoods need protection against speculative overdevelopment and that any future development must to be well located, proportionate, deliver infrastructure benefit, and affordable housing relevant to the needs of local people to benefit the generation to come.

With Tandridge being the only Surrey Council retaining an asset stock of Council Homes, Martin is keen this model is taken forward and the provision extended to improve control, value and investment for the New East Surrey Council.

As a local boy who attended Caterham school and has just completed a degree in Politics and International relations. I care deeply about our community and want to make a genuine change by cleaning up the Hill, improve our high street, tackle potholes and ensure residents feel proud and safe where they live.

New Reform councillors will bring a freshness and enthusiasm to longstanding problems of local Surrey towns. We will be practical, focused and responsible of local government finances. My main concern is that Caterham, like other Surrey towns is not focusing on improving local infrastructure, services and amenities.

Tony Pearce has called Caterham home for 30 years. He and his wife Lorna, to whom he has been happily married for 29 years, chose this community for its perfect balance – quick links to London, surrounded by beautiful countryside, with vibrant shops, restaurants and pubs right on the doorstep. Their son attends a local secondary school and enjoys the activities at the new youth club.

Tony is a familiar face to many across the area. He currently works at a well-known local community and wellbeing centre, where he supports people from all walks of life. Before that, he played a vital role during the pandemic – first volunteering, then serving as a volunteer coordinator at the local vaccine centre, and later working for the NHS at several vaccination sites. These experiences gave Tony a deep understanding of the needs and concerns of our community, especially those who feel left behind.

Community involvement has always been at the heart of Tony’s life. He and Lorna were both active on the PTA at Marden Lodge, helping organise events like the much-loved fireworks display. He also worked with local residents to protect green belt woodland from being sold off, safeguarding it for future generations. You’ll often find him and his family enjoying local events such as the food and Christmas markets, or joining in the fun at the carnival.

As your councillor, Tony will campaign hard to ensure that Caterham and Whyteleafe remain great places to live. He believes in the right housing in the right places, protecting green spaces, and making sure the town centre thrives with a wider range of retail. Above all, Tony wants to make sure no one in our community is left behind, and that everyone has access to the support they need.

Tony Pearce has the passion, experience, and commitment to continue the hardworking Liberal Democrat tradition in Caterham and Whyteleafe.

Robin grew up in Old Coulsdon and has called Caterham home for the past decade. With family on the Hill and home in the Valley, East Surrey is firmly at the centre of his life. Alongside his day job as a technology leader, where he helps organisations modernise and improve, Robin serves as a District Councillor for Harestone and as Vice Chair of Caterham Valley Parish Council. He is standing as a candidate for the East Surrey Unitary, Caterham Valley Division.

Robin believes public services work best when they are shaped around the outcomes that matter to local people. He takes a fair, practical, evidence-based approach, focused on local decision making and clear accountability. A keen walker, he cares deeply about local communities and green spaces, and believes it is possible to respond to a fast-paced world while holding on to what makes this area special.

As a Councillor, Robin has focused on making a difference locally. He has worked with Parish colleagues to secure funding to refurbish community spaces, including Timber Hill playground. He has led efforts to strengthen HMO enforcement and supported residents opposing large, unsustainable developments in Harestone. Robin recognises that residents are often passed between services, so he is focused on a more joined up approach, making it easier for people to access what they need.

Robin’s priorities are better day to day services, strong local representation, and a council residents can trust.

I’m standing as your Green Party candidate because I want to make our community fairer and more inclusive, a place where everyone can thrive.

Over the recent months, there has been an increase in hateful and divisive rhetoric within the UK that we have seen bleed into our local community; it is essential that we reject these ideas and rise together. However, in order to fight back against this division, our area has to feel like a community – this is only possible if our local council works with all of us to achieve this. From sports facilities and libraries to affordable housing and social care provision, we have opportunities to improve the lives of all residents and relight the fire at the heart of our community.

Vote hope, not hate. Vote Green.

It would be a privilege and honour to be re-elected to represent the residents of Oxshott, Stoke d’Abernon and Fairmile. I moved back to the area with my wife in 2019. It is where I grew up and love.

I have been in business all my life. People and  customer service have always been my priority from  starting at our family restaurant to CEO of a FTSE 100.

In 2010 I stepped away from executive life and became chairman or non-executive director of international companies in the USA, France, Middle East and UK.  I was chairman of the British Hospitality Association and awarded a CBE for services to industry. I was also proud to have chaired the governors of my old school.  Family is my core value. I have a grownup son and daughter, five grandchildren and two  stepsons.  

I pledge to use my commercial and people experience in the service of our community.

My vision

My work on behalf of all residents will be to support our unique community of the character villages of Oxshott and Stoke D’Abernon with Fairmile.

A place to live where people feel safe in their homes secure from outside intrusion, overcrowding and excessive through traffic. A place where good local services are normal.

I will do whatever is in my power:

To safeguard our Green Belt and environment policy and oppose out of character development.

To keep our local businesses strong and develop a positive social fabric.

To drive waste out from our Council spending and balance the books.

Local is my focus with no ambition other than to serve my friends and neighbours to the  very best of my ability.

Working with our excellent residents associations and colleagues I will be a voice for your  opinions to be heard and acted upon.

Together we will make a difference.

Stephen is currently Leader of Mole Valley District Council and a member of Surrey County Council. He is one of the most experienced Councillors in the country having served as an elected Councillor for 48 of the past 50 years - the 2 year gap was when a career change required a move from the North of England to the South. He has served on 5 different authorities including 8 years as Leader of the Liberal (Democrat) Group on Leeds City Council and 2 periods as Leader of the Group on Mole Valley Council.

He has been a member of Mole Valley Council representing Dorking South since 1992 and Council Leader since 2019, successfully steering the Mole Valley Group through the challenges of Council control; and has been a member of Surrey County Council representing Dorking South and the Holmwoods since 2005. Following a career in higher education, he retired from a senior post at the Open University in 2010 and has been a full time Councillor for the past 10 years.

He believes that being a member of both authorities is important because it allows a greater understanding of the role of each authority and a perspective on how the services provided by each compliments the other. It encourages co-operation rather than conflict which has to be of value to the community. Stephen is involved in a wide range of local organisations and has been able to help many community groups to progress their objectives.

I have lived in Dorking for over 37 years and have worked in all roles within IT including project management of million pound international projects, business analysis, quality assurance, and software development. I recently retired so I can now spend more time in volunteering and working on local issues. In the past I have volunteered nationally and locally for carers organisations, helped to set up a local mental health support group, supported local judo clubs, and was your Section Officer in the Police Specials in the late 90s. If elected as your unitary councillor I would ensure we have better provision for mental health, education, SEND, and local public transport. We also need to stop the building on our green belt and improve the quality and numbers of affordable housing in Dorking (Right Homes, Right Place Right Price).

Lisa was the district councillor for Charlwood & Hookwood prior to the 2023 boundary changes and has track record of positive, fast community action.

She has:

-Acted on speeding and antisocial driving, working with the community to introduce VAS signs
-Worked with Thames Water to stop sewage spills on to recreational land
-Campaigning to protect our Green Belt which is being inundated with speculative planning applications
-Introduced upgrading and clearing of pavements to keep pedestrians safe
-Initiated a review of flooding and water flow to better prepare for severe rainfall
-Supports and advises residents with energy saving measures and access to grants

I am proud to be standing as your Labour and Cooperative Party candidate. I have lived in Dorking with my family since 2008. It is a great place to call home and I care about what happens here. My career as a social worker has given me much insight into people’s lives and the challenges they face. The pandemic has exposed inequalities but also shown the generosity to help others that exists here. I believe we need to build back a greener and fairer society and if elected I commit to be an active and visible councillor.

Fred has lived in Dorking for the past 9 years, having previously grown up in Bookham. He is currently working as a Mechanical Engineer and is a newcomer to the political space.

His key policies Include:
- Protection of the natural environment and green spaces in and around Dorking
- Sustainable development of homes with infrastructure keeping pace
- Advocating for genuine upkeep of the local road network with an aim to reducing disruptive roadworks
- Forward the argument of practical solutions to traffic in Dorking including investigating the construction of a potential east/west bypass.

I have lived in Mole Valley for over 20 years, and I have been a resident in South Holmwood for the past 4 years.

I have two wonderful daughters, who have grown up in the area. My goal is to create a better, safer community in which we can all flourish.

For the past 30 years I have successfully managed and grown a local company, Employing people from the local area.

If elected I would be delighted to be your Council representative, and I would perform the role with common sense, responsibility, and energetic enthusiasm.

Born in Westcott, I love the beautiful unspoilt nature of our environment and wish to keep it that way. My concerns include traffic control, better bicycle lane provision, wider adoption of 20mph speed limits, and better public transport, as well as rethinking the town centre to ensure a vibrant environment maintaining local shops and the development of brownfield sites to help provide housing, instead of eroding the Green Belt. I am chair of the West Surrey Palestine Solidarity Campaign, and am active in support of various international political causes.

I have lived in Mole Valley for over 20 years, and I appreciate what a pleasant and safe environment it is to live in. However, like much of the South East, Mole Valley faces increasing pressures on its urban and green spaces.

The prospect of Gatwick expansion has now been compounded by the threat of fracking. These developments pose real threats to the quality of life in Mole Valley generally, and Dorking Hills specifically.

I believe that only the Green Party offers effective and coherent policies to develop Mole Valley, while maintaining it as a special place to live.

Standing as councillor in Westcott I hope to overturn the Liberal Democrat and Conservative dominance of Mole Valley. Labour has plans for a Green New Deal, which puts the environment at the heart of everything we do. I would push for improved local bus services, better recycling solutions and a review of parking services in Dorking to encourage diversity of businesses in the town. There should also be greater investment in Green technology, such as EV charging points. The closure of Sure Start children’s centres has been severely detrimental and a Labour council will seek to improve services for children.

I am standing as a Green Party candidate to bring practical, community-focused leadership to the local council.

As the founder of Immaculate Vegan, a successful ethical business, I understand the importance of balancing economic vitality with social and environmental responsibility.

I am passionate about protecting local services, supporting community wellbeing, and making the area a fairer, greener place for everyone. I believe local politics should be rooted in common sense, compassion, and long-term thinking — always putting residents and the local environment first.

I have lived in Mole Valley for the last thirty five years. I worked in IT and in risk management for the banking industry before retiring several years ago.
I am keen to see the Green Party take a much greater role in local affairs, and will be challenging and holding the council to account on spending AND maintaining Mole Valley’s unique character.

Neha has lived in Salfords since 2012, and locally in Reigate and Banstead since 2004 with her family. Neha started and leads the Asian Social Group which hosts community events and sports activities to help build community cohesion. After a career working in finance Neha has run her own business in Horley since 2016. Neha says, “I hope to make a difference helping residents access council services and address issues that affect their everyday lives – no matter how small.”

Robin has lived in Whitebushes for 22 years where he is part of the Whitebushes community group and runs the HelpintheBushes.co.uk community website. He also serves on the Raven Housing Trust’s customer voice panel, the local NHS foundation trust peoples’ panel and Surrey bus forum. He studied at East Surrey College and works locally.

I have worked as a care worker for the last twelve years, and as many things before then. I am divorced with four children and three grandchildren.

It was at the time of the Maastricht Treaty that I came to realise the true nature of the beast. I wrote to Sir George Gardiner, then MP for Reigate, promising my vote for him if he voted against the Treaty. He did, and I was more proud to vote for him in 1997 than on any previous visit to the ballot box.

Sir George introduced me to the Campaign for an Independent Britain, an umbrella group for EU-realists from all political parties. I soon realised the big three parties were fully signed up to the European Project, and there was nothing that their rank-and-file members could do about it.

Then I fell in with UKIP. I joined the party in 1999, and I have stood in elections at least once a year since 2000, sometimes twice a year, and three times in 2009. There have been many false dawns along the way, and we have learned just how deeply entrenched are the forces of compulsory integration. But we have also learned to fight harder, and never to be discouraged. I have joked in the past that I have lost count of how many elections I have contested, but not of how many I have won: none. But in May of this year, I came within a whisker of winning two elections.

Of course, I benefitted from the national swing to UKIP which has brought us so many victories and near misses this year, but I like to think that I have built up something of a local presence, with my relentless campaigning and letters in the local press, not to mention getting coverage for some of my less political activities. People sometimes recognise me in the street, and they usually have something supportive to say.

If we are to achieve our aims, we must broaden our appeal to those who once would have been considered natural Labour supporters. NuLab abandoned them, and they know it. A quarter of a century ago, Jacques Delors seduced British unions with promises

Chris wants a fair deal for Reigate. As a resident of more than ten years, he’s tired of the Conservative-run Surrey County Council not engaging with local communities and running services into the ground. If elected as your councillor in May, he will fight to make sure residents’ views are heard on the future of Reigate Priory School and other council-driven initiatives.

Chris is a local business owner and charity trustee. He is passionate about building a strong, inclusive community where every resident can thrive. He will campaign for a positive future for Reigate - protecting the environment and creating opportunities for young people from all backgrounds.

Having always voted and supported the Liberal Democrats local teacher Robin joined the party in 2002.  Since then he has campaigned for Lib Dems mayoral elections, served as a ward councillor in Wolverhampton and even stood for parliamentary elections.  He has recently moved to Surrey with his wife and three teenaged children and is keen to use his campaigning skills to help residents in South Park & Woodhatch.

Melissa settled in Epsom twelve years ago to raise her family. Before putting down roots in the area, Melissa lived and worked across Britain, making factual TV for the BBC. With a strong background in research, she brings a thoughtful, evidence-led approach to the issues that matter most locally. She is especially passionate about improving access to better quality mental healthcare for children and young people and about strengthening a sense of community across all walks of Epsom life so everyone feels supported and heard. Most days, you’ll find Melissa in the market square browsing the flower stalls.

Roy is a local business owner of a construction company, he also worked for many years in print manufacturing and creative media sectors and a dedicated father of two girls. He’s deeply involved in the Epsom community and believes strongly in giving back. When he’s not working, you’ll often find Roy enjoying a drink at his local, walking on the Downs watching the horses or supporting local charities, as a co-founder and chairperson of Surrey Stands With Ukraine. Roy is proud to call Epsom home and is passionate about helping the town not just survive, but thrive. He’s focused on reducing anti-social behaviour, creating the right conditions for families to flourish and supporting local businesses to grow, invest and succeed

candidates.p: Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell
candidates.stmt:
I moved into Woodcote in 2010 and quickly became involved with Friends of Rosebery Park, Epsom & Ewell Tree Advisory Board, and Woodcote Residents' Society.  First elected in 2019, my focus has been on planning and environment matters sitting on these related committees and driving change for improvements to our borough while fighting to keep Epsom & Ewell a beautiful place to live and work.

Having been a borough councilor for these past 4 years the time and work required are immense but rewarding.  Change is not fast and consistency with perseverance is key to achieving goals and deliverables.

Elected in 2019 I have focussed on planning and environment matters sitting on related committees.  I am standing again as I know I have even more to offer having spent the last 4 years working within the local government system.

My focus will be on working to provide the right type of housing in the right place, more trees, additional hedgerows, improved usage of our parks, fewer potholes, better roads, more school places, vibrant high-street, independent retailers, better support for small businesses, and an improved sense of community.

If elected I will continue to work hard to serve my residents and community.

candidates.p: Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell
candidates.stmt:
I live and work in the borough and attended the University for the Creative Arts in Ashley Road. As a borough councillor, I serve on Health Liaison, the H.R. Panel, Epsom & Walton Downs Conservators, and the Communities and Wellbeing committee. I’m Borough Heritage Champion, raising the profile of our history and supporting local and national charities through military band events at Epsom Playhouse.
My background is producing educational material for healthcare workers. This shapes my commitment to wellbeing, carers, and inclusive information for all ages. I listen, focus on practical solutions, and fight for independent, residents-first decision-making.
I have always enjoyed the countryside - I learned to ride here! I’m a volunteer water quality monitor with our local rivers’ trust - and recently, I successfully campaigned to create a new nature reserve in the borough.

I was brought up in Ewell and educated in South London.  I’m married with two grown-up daughters and now live in Leatherhead.  I have now retired after thirty years in the oil industry working in Europe, Latin America and Asia.
The whole country is demonstrably riddled with waste and incompetence and, after 60 years of pretty much uninterrupted control by the Conservatives, the hitherto one-party state which is Surrey is no exception.  The establishment parties will tell you that Reform candidates lack experience.  What they do not tell you is that their definition of experience is year after year of repeated failure to provide decent basic services and represent the interests of council-tax payers.

I’ve been a resident of Epsom and Ashtead area for nearly 40 years, and I write, paint and workout in my spare time. I’ve grown up with peace of mind regarding our environment, but in recent years, local plans to sell off and build on Green Belt land has made me extremely concerned, it is a dangerous path to cross. Weather conditions are changing; with stronger more intense downpours and hotter summers ahead. Also, the level of traffic increases year on year locally and around the M25. As your councillor, I will be committed to take action that will allow us to continue our lives with minimal change rather than facing a dramatic impact.

Kate Chinn is standing as one of the candidates in Town and Downs Ward. After qualifying as a mental health nurse Kate has had a long career working in London in mental health, health, housing and homelessness. She moved to the borough in the early 90s settling and raising her family here except for a brief period when the family moved to Delhi. In Delhi Kate worked in two camps in the city improving sanitation, with the help of the then DFID, as well as providing improved access to healthcare and education.

Kate was privileged to be elected as councillor in Court Ward in 2015 and re-elected twice has continued as a ward councillor. With her fellow councillors she is out on the doorsteps most 
weekends and present in the community listening to and working for the people she represents.

Kate welcomes the opportunity to be elected in the ward where she lives. Tackling the  indecision over the chalk pit which causes air pollution and noise as well and the lack of political direction with the local plan would be two of Kate’s priorities. Decisions which are 
needed have been pushed from pillar to post for years. Action is needed and the current representatives are part of the problem not the solution.

With a track record of enabling change Kate will work hard to get things done

I am Stuart Gosling, I have lived in Epsom for 14 years and in the East Surrey for most of my life. I recently retired from a career in IT which included working at many of the bigger companies in the area, and now work part time for a local community organisation.

I believe that only Labour can actually deliver improvements to the lives of ordinary people living in Epsom. For too long the RA have run Epsom and Ewell and have become complacent, taking the electors for granted.

With the move to a unitary authority there will be a lot of changes, now is the time to elect somebody who wants to use the opportunity to make life better for all those living in Epsom.

David lives in Horton with his family and has been at the heart of the local community for many years. He previously served as a councillor in the Horton area and as Chair of the Livingstone Park Residents Association, helping to bring people together and make residents’ voices heard. David has been closely involved in major local campaigns, including helping to establish the Horton Chapel Community Trust. He also serves as the CEO of a charity supporting disabled people, and is deeply committed to building a more inclusive society. When he’s not working, you’ll often find David walking his dog, Milo, in Horton Country Park, enjoying the green spaces that make Epsom such a special place to live. David is passionate about making Epsom West safe and welcoming, and about working with residents to deliver the changes the community needs.

John has had a long and successful career as a biology teacher in a local college. He has lived near Epsom Common for many years, where he has raised his family and become deeply involved in the local community. These days, John divides his time between working on his allotment, cycling around Epsom and volunteering locally. He is always happy to stop for a chat, and John’s priority is listening to residents and understanding the issues that matter most to them. He is passionate about preserving Epsom’s green spaces, supporting our young people and helping to build a community for everyone.

I have lived in Epsom for nearly 30 years and will work to preserve its unique character whilst remaining an attractive place for our young people to live with good education, employment and housing opportunities. 
I’ve spent over 25 years in legal and regulatory roles in the City of London, negotiating complex contracts, ensuring those contracted services are delivered and dealing with various public bodies. I will use that experience to hold local officials and contractors to account for the services they provide. 
I’m also a member of the Independent Monitoring Board, a statutory body which provides independent oversight of what happens in our prisons. This is a voluntary role and we are concerned with the fair and humane treatment of prisoners, and most importantly their rehabilitation prior to release. This role has provided valuable experience in helping men with difficult problems find solutions that will set them on a more productive path.
This is my first adventure into local politics, borne of a frustration with the mainstream parties, including a Residents Association that has signed up to the same “progressive” agenda, and a determination to get value for money for local tax payers.

Ben Cronin is a dedicated Reform UK candidate standing in the Epsom and Ewell local elections. With over 30 years’ experience delivering multimillion-pound IT projects and as the owner of his own small business, Ben brings real-world expertise and common sense to local politics. A proud father of four, he is deeply committed to protecting Epsom’s precious green spaces, slashing wasteful council spending, and fixing the basic infrastructure that residents rely on every day. Ben believes it’s time to put local people first and restore pride in our borough.

candidates.p: Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell
Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell

candidates.stmt: I’ve lived in Epsom West for 14 years, and am a keen open‑water swimmer. I previously worked as a programmer before helping to set up a local climate group, and later worked at the Ashtead refill shop. I’ve volunteered at Epsom repair café, and am currently helping launch a Library of Things. I joined the Green Party to push for change from within, and have been involved in organising political discussion groups and campaigning to protect our Green Belt sites. Over the last year I’ve felt very privileged to listen to Epsom West residents’ concerns on their doorsteps.

I have lived in Epsom with my family since 2007. I’m a qualified environmental engineer with over 30 years of experience working in the UK and internationally to improve municipal services focusing on drainage, waste management and pollution control. Through my experience working with local authorities, civil society organisations and private sector organisations, I understand the importance of consultation and participatory planning to meet local needs. As your councillor on the new East Surrey Unitary Authority, I will listen to and respond to your priorities, and work with other elected officials to ensure that Epsom West remains an attractive and vibrant area to live.

It would be a privilege to represent the good people of Epsom West ward on the new East Surrey Council. I know the area quite well as I have lived in Epsom for 24 years and campaigned all over the borough. We'll need to speak up for ourselves and our issues; this is not a time to be faint-hearted.
As an active retired nurse my priorities are the things that the County Council is responsible for which affect people's daily lives: social care, education, highways, trading standards, transport, recreation etc. I volunteer at our local park to keep it tidy and welcoming and would strive to keep our local green areas as respite and safety from our busy lives.
My children went to state schools and have done well; school is a massive part of a child's life; teaching staff deserve every support and credit. Children get one shot at childhood and that needs to be a positive experience to set them on the road as responsible adults.
Comprehensive social care is still an aspiration, but getting the best value for money, rather than the cheapest option should be the role of the Council, and supporting carers properly is common sense.

With over 40 years of dedicated service as an NHS manager and agency lead, I bring a lifetime of leadership, compassion, and practical problem-solving experience to our community. Now a retired 75-year-old, I remain deeply active within my political party and find great fulfillment in volunteering for several local charities. My career has been defined by a commitment to public service and the well-being of others, qualities I am eager to channel into representing our residents with the same kindness, politeness, and friendly approachable nature that I have carried throughout my professional life.
 
On a personal note, I am the proud father of two children and grandfather of five grandchildren and am inspired daily by my wife, a University lecturer with a doctorate in Nursing. Our family’s deep roots in healthcare and education reinforce my belief in the power of community support and lifelong learning. I am running for councillor because I believe my extensive experience in management and my heart for charitable work make me uniquely qualified to listen to your concerns and advocate for a better, more inclusive future for everyone in our ward.

Hinchley Wood, Claygate and Oxshott is a special place and it is one of the best in Surrey to live, work, retire and raise a family.

That’s why Andrew Burley standing to be your next County Councillor: to protect what makes our villages great and fight for the services local people rely on.

We are having this by-election after the sad passing of Cllr Mark Sugden. Mark was the very definition of a community champion, respected by everyone, regardless of politics. I’m honoured to have the chance to follow in his footsteps.

But this election also matters for the future.

The Liberal Democrats are already making damaging decisions at Borough level by cutting Meals on Wheels, shutting down community centres, and failing to deliver a local plan that protects our green spaces. We cannot afford to let them gain more ground at County Hall.

Dear Resident,

My name is Amanda. I am a married local mother to two teenagers, and when I'm not busy with them I follow my passion of helping to secure better futures for local people.

I have been volunteering and working in Esher for the past 11 years. Helping out at the Lower Green Community Centre, this led to me becoming the Lower Green community facilitator, engaging local people with local projects. I helped start the East Elmbridge foodbank and jointly ran a community interest company called Kingdom Fruit - which successfully sold fruit and veg to local people and local businesses.

As a Trustee of Lower Green Community CIO I continue to work with the local community. Currently I am a teaching assistant at a local primary school, which is very enjoyable and rewarding. Prior to all of this I was a property lawyer.

I believe in fairness, compassion and integrity.

With passion and drive we can achieve a lot, and I have witnessed this working with many amazing local people in Esher. I would love to represent Esher, Lower Green, West End and Hersham to bring positive change and new opportunities.

Yours sincerely,

 Amanda Manship 

Local Conservative Candidate for Esher

Time for something new.I have lived in Claygate all my life and am actively involved in the community as a member of the CVA Committee, the Claygate Lights Committee, and the Claygate Parish Council.

Our community is under increasing pressure. A lack of proper local planning is attracting developers in large numbers, and if the current proposals go ahead, our neighbourhoods will be fundamentally changed.

The time has come to stand up for our community and to protect our local Green Belt.

I have lived in Surrey for almost a decade, and it is a pleasure to live in a prosperous county that has some of the best schools, innovations, industries, and sights.

However, the constant rise in taxing, whilst public services are declining, and our infrastructure flooding, will only continue and get worse under the other parties.

Only Reform UK can bring much needed change

Ian has lived in Claygate for over 30 years. Since his retirement he has been a trustee of the local Recreation Ground, helping the local community make the most of the green space in the centre of the village.
He is a keen runner and can often be seen out running on the paths in the woods around the village. Ian has organised the much-loved annual Claygate Country 5 run for the last 3 years.
As a councillor I will seek practical solutions to address the community's problems and issues. Everyone should be treated fairly and have equal opportunity to 
access to the services and infrastructure they need. This is underpinned by our mutual need to avoid harming the environment and nature.

I’ve proudly called Claygate home for the past two years. After living in Twickenham, Norfolk and Sydney (Aus) Claygate has really stood out as a fantastic place to live offering the perfect blend of a village feel with immediate access to our great capital city.
This proximity does though present challenges to the residents of Claygate, Esher and Oxshott. Something that stood out when I first moved here was the persistent issue of fly-tipping in and around the area. It is unacceptable, and I will push for stronger enforcement, better reporting systems, and real accountability.
I will also apply sustained pressure to ensure a significant improvement in the upkeep of our local roads. Residents deserve safe, well-maintained streets, not patchwork fixes.
Claygate, Esher and Oxshott deserves action, not excuses and that’s exactly what I’m here to deliver.
Through my work as a contract manager in the housing sector, I understand the real, everyday challenges our communities face particularly in the face of unsustainable, irresponsible development by adjacent London Boroughs.
I am standing for the Social Democratic Party (SDP) as I really do believe they are the only party that offer thought out, considered solutions to the problems Britain faces today. I urge anybody reading this to take a minute and check out the SDP website.
If like me you have become disillusioned with the offerings from the status quo then the SDP may just be the party for you.

candidates.p: Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell
Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell

candidates.stmt: Andrew has lived in the local area for eighteen years and is a proud Irishman who has built strong ties within the community. Now retired, he is actively involved in local life, volunteering at the foodbank and supporting local initiatives. He wants to see better public services, greater financial competence from those in power and real hope for a better future. He is standing to give residents across Ewell Court, Auriol and Cuddington a fair deal. A lover of music and cinema, Andrew can often be found enjoying a good film or live music and, when he’s not doing that, he’ll likely be watching the rugby or horse racing.

Jeanne loves the local area and is proud to call it home, having moved here from Hong Kong and quickly becoming part of the community. She is an IT professional with over twelve years’ experience working on government projects, specialising in security and audit. She sings weekly in her church choir and enjoys travelling, swimming, cycling and cooking for friends and family. Jeanne is standing for Ewell Court, Auriol and Cuddington because she wants to bring fairness and a strong community voice to local decision-making, ensuring everyone in the community is heard. She is a Liberal Democrat because of the party’s strong support for Hongkongers and its rational approach to politics.

Having lived in the Epsom and Leatherhead area for 14 years, and with both my children attending Therfield School, I feel deeply connected to the vibrant local community. I’ve been a civil servant since 1992, working primarily in environmental departments and now for Defra focusing on animal and plant health and welfare policy. I’m committed to improving the standard of living for all residents, with a particular focus on creating opportunities and support for young people. I believe in a fairer, greener future for Epsom and Ewell — one where nature is protected, public services are strengthened, and every resident can thrive.

I have lived in Epsom for almost 50 years and joined the Green Party in 2015 because of a desire to change the tide of the political environment and to prioritise the needs of our citizens over that of the elites. Over the years little appears to have changed, until now. The Green Party is spreading a new and hopeful voice that will improve democracy, reduce inequality and protect the environment. I believe a strong Green Party presence at a local level will drive change from the bottom up. We know what to do, let’s try and do it.

Richard Chinn is standing as one of the candidates in Ewell Court, Auriol and Cuddington Ward:
Richard joined the Royal Navy after leaving school and trained as an engineer. He left and held various engineering posts. After completing a masters engineering degree Richard came to know Epsom when he started working for a large engineering company in the area in the late 80’s. He realised what a lovely place it would be to live and moved to the borough in the early 1990s settling and raising his family. He has travelled extensively with his work including moving with his family to Delhi for four years. Now, soon to be retiring Richard would welcome the opportunity to work in and with his local community. 
Married to Kate who has been a councillor in the borough for 11 years Richard has a good understanding of what the role entails. Richard has gone out with the Court Ward Cllrs supporting them talking to residents to identify any support needed to address issues within the area.

Additionally, he has a good understanding of the challenges facing residents, the cost of living, ASB 
and the current shockingly poor state of the roads. Richard will work to support residents liaising with other services and holding the new East Surrey council to account to ensure they provide an excellent service for their residents

Julian is a serving councillor for Epsom & Ewell Borough Council and has many years of experience across different committees and local initiatives, giving him a deep understanding of how local government works. He also has a background in social housing, which began with feeding the homeless in London, shaping his long-standing commitment to supporting vulnerable people. Professionally, he has worked in IT,  bringing practical problem-solving skills to his work in the community. He lives in Ewell Village and is actively involved in his church community, volunteering locally. When he’s not doing that, you may find him spending time with his children as a dedicated family man. Julian is committed to being part of a fresh start for Ewell Village, Stoneleigh, Nonsuch, and East Surrey, standing with a party that genuinely cares about listening to residents and making sure services run properly.

candidates.p: Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell
Residents Associations of Epsom and Ewell

candidates.stmt: I’ve grown up in Epsom and Ewell and feel fiercely protective of this neighbourhood. Its beautiful parks, charming town centre and phenomenal schools are reasons I chose to keep my roots here. I would work hard for the people of this ward as councillor. I am a strong believer that if you raise up the marginalised in society, we all rise up with them. I have studied law and worked to provide legal aid to asylum seekers. I currently work within a trade union. I would bring the compassion and intelligence required in this line of work to advocate for each and every one of our neighbours.

I’ve been a jobbing Actor all my working life, and I’ve lived in this area for over twenty years. I came because of family, and stayed because I feel rooted in the community. I’m standing for election because I believe we have more power than we think, especially in times when we often feel as though we have no power at all. I’ll try to make a difference for all of us. Don’t feel like you have to vote tactically. The Green Party has shown we can win anywhere. Vote Green on May 7th, and let’s get things done together.

Helen has lived in Epsom & Ewell for more than 40 years. She has worked locally in Public Health, and secondary and post 16 education. She campaigns locally for environmental sustainability and equal opportunities across the whole age range. She is currently focussing on pedestrian safety and increased priority for pavement repair.

I am a retired company director having managed my own electronics business for 30 years.  I moved to Epsom in 2013 and was immediately impressed with the drive and enthusiasm of the local Labour Party members.  From a historic position of a minor player both in council seats and in the parliamentary constituency, Labour’s presence in the area has grown substantially over this period.  Holding the Mayoralty recently was a suitable reward for such endeavour.  
 
If honoured by election for the Ewell, Stoneleigh and Nonsuch ward, I will be guided by Labour’s principles of inclusivity, fairness, and opportunity for all in representing you in local government.  The effects on international relationships from war, tariffs, and Brexit have led to a difficult political and economic climate for the country.  It is local government’s role to mitigate this where possible.

I feel privileged to be standing for election as the Liberal Democrat candidate for the Godstone Ward. I have lived in the area all my life, was educated in schools in Warlingham and moved to South Godstone with my husband 30 years ago. I have raised my family here and am proud that my eldest daughter works as a Quantity Surveyor in Caterham, while my youngest daughter, is now at the University of Leeds. My husband, Duncan, runs a local business, and is involved in local community groups. I am passionate about protecting the character of this area and maintaining our local community services.

Roben has recently moved to the area after getting engaged and is now building his life here, proud to call Tandridge home. He has worked as an administrative assistant in Nutfield and has been involved in local campaigns, giving him a clear understanding of the issues that matter to residents across Godstone, Bletchingley, Nutfield and the surrounding villages. He is committed to protecting what makes our villages special and to supporting activities that strengthen our local communities.

Roben’s professional background has focused on practical, evidence-based work that supports people in their day-to-day lives. He is particularly concerned about the growing number of potholes on our roads, the pressures facing children and families in the SEND system, and the impact of new housing when it is not matched by the roads, services and infrastructure our area needs. He believes decisions should be made with residents, not imposed on them, and wants to see a fairer, more open approach to local government. If elected, he will work to push back against inappropriate development, protect village character, and secure proper investment in local services, while being a visible and approachable councillor who listens and acts on residents’ concerns.

**Standing up for Hersham, Burwood Park, and Whiteley Village**

I want to be straight with residents: whoever takes control of the new East Surrey Council will inherit a very difficult financial position. The government's new funding formula is costing Surrey more than £180 million - money that would otherwise pay for roads, schools, and adult social care. Surrey County Council has already built up nearly £50 million in reserves and put a recovery plan in place. That's the kind of careful management Hersham needs, and I won't make promises that can't be kept.

Even as opposition councillors and against a difficult financial backdrop, the local Conservative team has worked with the community to deliver:

**Potholes and roads**. Working with Councillor John O'Reilly, we launched the Hersham Pothole Patrol that's seen hundreds of potholes repaired, and the resurfacing of Queens Road is now underway. Important progress - but there's more to do.

**Hersham Community Centre and Meals on Wheels**. We continue to fight for both after Elmbridge ended them. These should remain community assets we can all use.

**Bringing an end to overdevelopment**. We stood with residents and the local community to defeat the seven-storey tower block plans. Next, we'll work with Waitrose to shape the future of the neglected Hersham shopping centre which has too many empty units.

**A bigger GP surgery**. We're backing the larger GP surgery Hersham urgently needs at the Technology Park / Air Products site, so residents can get an appointment when they need one.

**Schools for every child**. We supported the new Hopescourt special needs school, funded by the last government, and the expansion of Walton Leigh - so every local child has a good school close to home.

**The choice on polling day**

Elmbridge has had a Liberal Democrat council since 2019, and we now have a Labour government cutting Surrey's funding. At the last local elections, the Conservatives came a very close second in Hersham

My name is Harrison, and I have proudly called Hersham home my entire life. For the past four years, I have served as a councillor in Elmbridge, where I have seen firsthand the failures of both the Conservatives and Liberal Democrats.

I have stood up for residents, secured funding for local schools and charities, and opposed unpopular developments like the Hersham Centre plans because I believe our community deserves better.

What is clear in this election is that it will be a tight race between real change or more of the same failure. Having worked alongside both the Conservatives and Liberal Democrats on the council, I can tell you it often feels like two sides of the same coin. That is why Hersham feels no different, regardless of which of them is in charge.

This could be Hersham’s last chance to take a different path. It is time for real, lasting, genuine change.

I will continue to stand up for residents, fight for proper infrastructure alongside development, and protect our Green Belt and the character of the place we all call home.

A Local Voice with Real-World Experience
I have lived in this area for 35 years. My daughter attended our local schools, and my family has long benefited from the fantastic facilities and community centres that make Hersham special. I am not a career politician; in fact, I have never been political before. However, I am opinionated when it comes to the future of our community, and I believe it is time for a change.

40 Years of Business Leadership
I am standing for Reform UK because I believe our council needs the "common-sense" approach that only real-world experience can provide. I have spent 40 years in business as a company director, starting multiple high-technology businesses and managing large-scale companies. I know how to bring organisational clarity and rigorous fiscal management to complex projects—skills that are desperately needed as we face the challenges of the new unitary authority.

Listening to You, Not Social Media
I don’t do social media. I prefer the old-fashioned way: speaking directly to people. In this campaign alone, I have knocked on over 4,000 doors.

On the doorstep, the issues are clear:

• Protecting our Green Belt: Stopping the overdevelopment that threatens the character of Hersham.

• Restoring Community Centres: Reversing the decimation of the local hubs that bring us together.

• Supporting our Children: Fighting for the proper support and resources for children with Special Educational Needs (SEND).

• Fixing the Basics: Addressing the neglected state of our roads and the endless cycle of potholes.

Common Sense for Hersham
The current council often lacks basic empathy and common sense. Recently, I spoke with a blind resident who was told by the council to "send her request in by text." This is the kind of bureaucratic nonsense that happens when councillors lose touch with reality. Which of today’s councillors has championed her issue?

As your councillor, I will manage the upcoming challenges of the new unitary authority with loc

Barry is fighting hard for a better deal for Horley residents. The Conservatives have wrecked education in Horley and only the Liberal Democrats can fix it! Barry was born and brought up in Horley and wants to see better opportunities, educational levels and support for the next generation.

Barry is passionate about his community and has been speaking to people across Horley about the issues affecting us all. Horley has always been forgotten by the Conservatives in Surrey County Council and enough is enough. His experience in business means he can hold the County Council to account for how badly they treat parents and will work hard to get a fair deal for Horley.

Hello, I’m Gary Bates and I’m standing as one of the Reform UK candidates for Horley East.

I live locally and, like many residents, I care deeply about how decisions are made, and whether local people are actually being listened to.

I’m standing because I believe we need more common-sense, transparency, and accountability at council level.

Too often decisions feel distant from the people they affect. I want to help change that by focusing on practical solutions that work for residents, not political box-ticking.

Oliver, a proud Horley resident and parent of five, shares this commitment to strengthening the town’s future. With a passion for local arts and culture, he supports efforts to boost creativity and bring renewed energy to Horley’s town centre. He is also a strong advocate for using technology to bridge the digital divide, helping ensure that every resident—regardless of age or background—has access to the tools and opportunities needed in a modern world.

I grew up in Horley and work in Finance at East Surrey Hospital. Through my professional experience, I will work to ensure that the council’s budgets are used efficiently, to keep council tax as low as possible.

I have been a town councillor since 2011 and have represented Horley at county-wide events. I am on the Horley Royal British Legion committee, organising local remembrance events, and Horley Town Management Group, a group of stakeholders working to improve Horley.

I also volunteer regularly for the Greyhound Trust doing dog walking and short-term fostering.

During my 15 years on Horley Town Council, I have an average attendance at 95% of meetings and, if elected to East Surrey Council, I intend to be equally diligent about my attendance there too.

**Do Horley a favour, vote Samantha and Taylor**

I live in Ringley Avenue, and care passionately about making our town a better place to live. So when residents asked me to put myself forward to be one of your East Surrey Councillors, I wanted to do so.

I have a track record of action in the community, despite not being an elected representative. I've reported over 400 potholes to Surrey Highways since May last year, I tackled fly tipping in Riverside Garden Park, put pressure on Network Rail to cut back overgrown hedges around Horley Station and have relentlessly campaigned to get our town the infrastructure it needs.

If you elect Samantha and I to East Surrey Council, our priorities are:

- **Fix the potholes and pavements**. We'll get the worst roads in Horley East fully resurfaced in our first term if elected.
- **Make Horley feel safe again**. We'll work with Surrey Police & Crime Commissioner Lisa Townsend and local police to tackle crime and anti-social behaviour.
- **Get Horley what we need for the future**. Our town is growing and we haven't caught up. We'll clear the drains and fix the mobile reception in Langshott and The Acres.
- **Support our local businesses**. By pushing for one hour of free parking in Council-owned car parks and a six month rates rebate for all new businesses in the town centre.
- **More school places**. Pushing for better school provision, including a second secondary school. And delivering a proper plan based on my lived experience as a former SEND pupil to make SEND education better.
- **Keep Horley clean and tidy**. By tackling fly tipping, installing more bins in the Gardens Estate, and campaigning to stop pavement parking on double yellow lines.

On Thursday 7 May, you have the opportunity to shape Horley's future for the next few years, and your vote could make all the difference.

This election won't change what's happening in Westminster, despite what's being said on some leaflets. But we can change what happens here in H

I have lived in Horley for nearly 20 years, raised my family here, and been an active member of the community through my various interests in the arts, fitness and conservation. I currently works in social housing, managing retrofit projects to make homes more energy efficient. Horley and Salfords are friendly, enterprising communities, but we often feel overlooked, and our green spaces are becoming increasingly threatened. Voting Green in Horley would bring some fresh ideas and a new voice to challenge the long-standing conservative-led council. If elected I would work with you to hold those in power to account, ensure our views and needs are taken into consideration, and strive to make our town an even better place.

**Vote Steel and Victoria and make hope normal again**

I’m Steel Horton, I live in Langshott and I’m standing for you in the new East Surrey Council because I believe Horley and our wider community deserve leaders who listen, who act with integrity, and who are ready to get stuck in to solve the real problems we face every day.

For too long, we’ve seen neglect, empty promises, and a system that feels disconnected from our needs. Whether it’s crumbling roads, unsafe streets, a lack of affordable homes, or the urgent climate crisis, we know that change is possible, but it requires experience, courage, and a genuine commitment to our local area.

As your Green Party candidate, I’ll work to deliver the change that we need. My vision is simple: a place where every resident has access to the services, infrastructure, and financial stability they need to thrive.

**Here is what Victoria and I will fight to achieve if elected:**

- **Fix Our Roads and Make Streets Safer:** I will push for better maintenance, introduce 20mph zones around schools, and expand safe cycling and walking routes to protect our families.
- **Build Genuinely Affordable Homes:** I will prioritise socially rented housing to address waiting lists while fiercely protecting our Green Belt and ensuring new developments come with the schools, doctors, and transport we actually need.
- **Lead on Climate Action:** From retrofitting homes to lower energy bills to decarbonising council operations by 2030, I will make climate action the heart of every decision.
- **Invest in Our Families and Vulnerable Residents:** I will work to expand children’s centres, improve mental health support in schools, and ensure our adult social care is person-centred and dignified.
- **Bring Services Back In-House:** I believe in holding power to account. I will work to bring key services like waste management and bus operations back under local control to improve quality and accountability.
- **Divest and Invest Ethically:** I w

Steve moved to the Borough nine years ago with his family, following five years spent working abroad in Asia. He has three children at local schools and works in banking and financial services, commuting 2-3 days a week into the City. If elected, Steve's priorities would be: building more homes, including affordable houses for first time buyers; enhancing the borough's transition to net zero (through good green transport and EV charging); and improving opportunities for school leavers and young adults. Steve joined the Lib Dems in 2018 to campaign against Brexit, he is a committed pro-European and supports restoring our relationship with Europe.

Andrew lives in Horley with his family. With a career in IT and lived experience as an Autistic parent, he understands the challenges many families face. He is standing to improve support for SEND families, ensure residents are listened to, and help local services work more effectively for those who rely on them.

*Meet Michael Blacker*

Michael is standing to be your East Surrey Councillor for Horley West, Salfords & Sidlow. He runs a consulting engineering business locally, so understands the pressures facing local businesses.

He's an highly experienced Borough Councillor, who has served the borough of Reigate & Banstead since 1979 (with some breaks in between terms). He has served as a Town Councillor for Horley South since 2023. He's passionate about public service and is a champion for Horley West, Salfords & Sidlow.

Working with Christian Stevens, his priorities include bringing his planning expertise to ensure any development in our communities has the appropriate infrastructure before it's built. He's supportive of getting more amenities for Westvale Park including shops and a new community centre. Along with his other Conservative colleagues across Horley, he also supports a new, second secondary school for the town.

He also cares passionately about making sure Horley West, Salfords & Sidlow has a strong voice at the new East Surrey Council. He and Christian will make sure that all of our communities are represented well.

*Meet Christian Stevens*

Christian is standing to be your Eastern Surrey Conservatives Councillor for Horley West, Salfords & Sidlow.

He has ten years of experience serving Horley as a Borough Councillor for Horley Central and South, from 2014 to 2024. He is proud to have served the town where he grew up, went to school, worked and still lives in now. He spent the first six months of his life in Court Lodge, in the now demolished Rutherwick Tower. His mother grew up on the estate and attended the former Court Lodge Comprehensive. So Christian's roots in Horley and passion for our community run deep. You can find a track record of Christian's service by visiting his old Councillor Facebook page at Former Cllr Christian Stevens.

Working with Michael Blacker, Christian's priorities for Horley West, Salfords & Sidlow include making sure Westvale Park has the facilities it needs to thrive. This includes securing shops, building a local pub to foster a greater sense of community and also supporting the new community centre. He also supports a new secondary school for Horley, which the town so badly needs. This is agreed by all Horley Conservative candidates.

He is also keen to once again work with fellow Horley Conservatives councillors and Surrey Police & Crime Commissioner Lisa Townsend PCC to tackle crime and anti-social behaviour.

When he was previously a Borough Cllr, he was a prominent member of the Planning Committee for many years and supported the opening of the new Lidl on Brighton Road.

He looks forward to working hard on your behalf and earning your trust to allow him to serve our communities once again.

Alan says, “I am honoured to stand as a Liberal Democrat candidate to represent Leatherhead and Fetcham East in the new East Surrey Council. 
“I am passionate about good quality housing, built in the right place with proper infrastructure. I want to revitalise our town centre, bring opportunities for local businesses and create a vibrant community that we can all be proud of.
“We will need a strong voice in the new East Surrey Council. I will ensure that your voice is heard.”

Jolanta says, "I am standing for the new unitary authority to help build a fairer, brighter future and create real opportunities for young people. I will work for greater transparency, better access to suitable education, and stronger support to reduce NEET numbers. I will also prioritise SEND provision, social care, and mental health services to build a more inclusive community.
"Having lived in the UK for 35 years and as a mum of two sons with special and complex needs, I understand the barriers families face in accessing support.
"I bring experience working in finance within an education-focused organisation, giving me a clear understanding of funding pressures in SEND and education, and the need for fair and effective use of resources.”
I'm standing for Reform UK to end wasteful spending, restore sound public finances, protect the Greenbelt, and deliver safer streets for our communities.

I have lived in Leatherhead for the past five years and am proud to call it home. I believe our community deserves a hopeful future shaped by fresh ideas and more young voices in local politics. As a Green councillor, I would campaign to protect our green spaces, improve walking, cycling and public transport, support local businesses, and push for genuinely affordable housing. The Green Party is championing bold action to tackle both the rising cost of living and the climate crisis. I want to bring that same ambition locally – standing up for our community, the local environment and helping create a fairer, greener future for Leatherhead.

I joined the Green Party because environmentalism has been a part of my life since primary school all the way up to my adult life. I still remember the buzz of the Eco-Schools meetings and how that early hands-on experience, from energy monitoring to school garden projects, stayed with me all the way through secondary school. It really shaped how I see the world, teaching me early on that small, local changes actually lead to a much bigger impact.

I am very pleased to be your Labour and Co-operative candidate. Many of you will know me as a vocal campaigner for the town and the High Street in particular. I organised events such as the carnival and Picnic on the Plaza and will do so again.

Covid 19 exposed the vulnerabilities in our community, and I applaud all of you in the NHS and those who supported the old, the disabled, isolated people and the hungry. I think it is important to build on that experience, recognise our strengths and support the weakest.

We are fortunate to live in the Surrey Hills we appreciate and want to protect the environment. I believe there is a bright future to be built on new investment the builds on green industry and innovation to create new opportunities for us all to enjoy happier healthier productive lives and build a more caring society together.

As your councillor I will fight tirelessly for better maintenance of our roads, road improvement and much better facilities for cyclists and pedestrians. I will also fight to raise the standard of social care and other services. And all this will demand better funding of the council – and I will be there to fight for that.

I am a retired District Judge and Civil Registrar, Solicitor (non-practising). Living in Lingfield. Local issues need addressing properly and efficiently by East Surrey. I will be the voice that puts our community first.

I have been an East Surrey resident for over 30 years. I am a newly appointed magistrate and business owner. East Surrey has declined. I want to live in safety, with quick local healthcare, save the green belt and help businesses.

Dear East Surrey Residents,                                                                                           I am delighted to be standing as the Independent Candidate in the forthcoming East Surrey Unitary Election. For the past two years, in my own time and at my own expense, I have attended many meetings and conferences in the UK covering political topics including, this year: Mending Britain's Broken Economy; The Freedom Festival at Buckingham University; Spectator events; The Rule of Law; and Free Trade; a Gala Dinner at London Guildhall and a Policy Summit discussing Border Force in Yorkshire. These events are useful for keeping my thinking current and, gave me the opportunity to meet councillors who are already in a unitary council and who were willing to answer my questions on how a unitary council works in their experience.  
Born in Hampshire, I grew up in the leafy New Forest District. I have lived in Tandridge for 41 Years and have always worked with my local community, initially in a NHS hospital pharmacy, as a part-time Head Gymnastics Coach at my local sports' centre for 34 years and as a Primary Teacher in Surrey and Sussex. I specialised in teaching Global Education, PSHE and Sport. The British Council enabled me to visit a remote village school in the Gambia and I was one of ten teachers who went to Mauritius to research Citizenship.
I first became interested in politics 11 years ago and have been passionately involved ever since. I stood in European, local and Parliamentary elections before becoming an Independent District Councillor for Felbridge (2021-24) where I sat on Planning and Planning Policy Committees, Gatwick New Runway and Community Infrastructure Working Groups. My approach is not party-led but issue-led.  I am genuinely interested in people and politics and I appreciate the natural world. I have no political party ties and would be working only for the residents of our villages and our countryside.
Since May 2024, I have con

Richard is a retired Building Services Engineer, married to Anne, with two adult children. He has lived in the district since 1991. In May 2024 he was elected to Tandridge District Council representing Bletchingley & Nutfield ward.
